original_abstract:
We report the pressure-dependent kinetics of the simplest Criegee intermediate, CH₂OO, reacting with SO₂, NO₂, NO, H₂O, and CH₃CHO, alongside the reaction of CH₂I radicals with NO₂ at 295 K. Using flash photolysis of CH₂I₂–O₂–N₂ mixtures, we generated CH₂OO and monitored its behavior under pseudo-first-order conditions. Reaction kinetics were followed by tracking formaldehyde (HCHO) products through laser-induced fluorescence (LIF) and, for the SO₂ system, via direct detection of CH₂OO using photoionization mass spectrometry (PIMS).

Our results show that the rate coefficients for CH₂OO reacting with SO₂ and NO₂ are independent of pressure over the ranges studied, yielding values of (3.42 ± 0.42) × 10⁻¹¹ cm³ s⁻¹ (1.5–450 Torr) and (1.5 ± 0.5) × 10⁻¹² cm³ s⁻¹ (25–300 Torr), respectively. In contrast, the reaction between CH₂OO and CH₃CHO exhibits a clear pressure dependence; as pressure increases, the HCHO yield drops, suggesting the stabilization of a secondary ozonide. We also established stringent upper limits for the reactions of CH₂OO with NO (< 2 × 10⁻¹³ cm³ s⁻¹) and H₂O (< 9 × 10⁻¹⁷ cm³ s⁻¹). Notably, the upper limit for the CH₂OO + H₂O reaction is significantly lower than previously reported values. This finding suggests that the atmospheric removal of CH₂OO by water vapor is less efficient than once thought, which has important implications for accurately modeling the role of Criegee intermediates in tropospheric oxidation and aerosol formation.
humanized_abstract:
The pressure dependent kinetics of the title reaction have been investigated for the simple Criegee biradical CH2OO reacting with SO2, NO2, NO, H2O and CH3CHO as well as for the reaction of CH2I with NO2 at 295 K. Flash photolysis of mixtures of CH2I2–O2–N2 was used to generate CH2OO and under pseudo first order conditions the reaction was probed by monitoring the formation of HCHO product(s) via laser-induced fluorescence (LIF) and also for the SO2 reaction by direct detection of CH2OO by means of photoionization mass spectrometry (PIMS).

The rate coefficients for the reactions of CH2OO with SO2 and NO2 were found to be pressure independent over the ranges investigated, yielding (3.42 ± 0.42) × 10−11 cm3 s−1 between 1.5 and 450 Torr and (1.5 ± 0.5) × 10−12 cm3 s−1 between 25 and 300 Torr, respectively. The reaction between CH2OO and CH3CHO was found to exhibit a pressure dependence with increasing yield of HCHO at low pressure and with the amount of HCHO falling off at higher pressure consistent with the formation of a secondary ozonide. Stricter upper limits to the rate coefficients for the reaction of CH2OO with NO and H2O were also derived: <2 × 10−13 cm3 s−1 and <9 × 10−17 cm3 s−1, respectively. In particular, the upper limit for this latter reaction is 2 orders of magnitude below all previously reported literature values, and the consequent major reduction in the removal of CH2OO in atmospheric water vapour has profound implications for modelling the pathways of oxidation of Criegee intermediates in the troposphere as well as aerosol formation.

document.output:
